71	This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.

file.content:
The iptables backend is pinned to legacy via `update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy` in the Dockerfile.
This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.

===== Slim Bottle Vessel

The tadmor bottle's runtime function is a socat echo service on port 8888.
The minimal bottle vessel requires only `socat`.

===== Ifrit Bottle Vessel (`rbev-bottle-ifrit`)

The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `nodejs` + `npm`
| Claude Code runtime (Claude Code is a Node.js application)

| `python3-scapy`
| Arbitrary packet construction, raw socket access, protocol-level probing.
Scapy is the single most dangerous tool for a creative attacker — it exposes every capability that dedicated tools like nmap, hping3, and iodine provide, but programmatically, allowing an AI agent to craft and iterate on attacks at machine speed.

| `strace`
| Syscall boundary mapping — lets the agent observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
Hard to replicate in pure Python.

| `bash`, `curl`, `git`
| Claude Code operational dependencies
|===

toolUseResult.content:
# Ifrit (tether variant) — in-bottle attack binary for Recipe Bottle crucible testing
#
# Single-stage build: Rust toolchain stays in image (test vessel, size
# irrelevant, iteration speed matters). Layer-cached dependency compilation
# means source-only changes rebuild just the ifrit crate.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/ (shared between tether and
# airgap variants — companion Dockerfile.airgap builds FROM a pre-warmed forge
# image to satisfy airgap egress discipline).

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Avoid prompts from apt
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install network attack tools (load-bearing for security boundary probes)
# socat:          Echo service on port 8888 (entry port security tests)
# dnsutils:       dig/nslookup for DNS security tests
# netcat-openbsd: nc for TCP connectivity tests
# traceroute:     ICMP path verification tests
# strace:         Syscall boundary mapping from inside the container
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    socat           \
    dnsutils        \
    netcat-openbsd  \
    traceroute      \
    strace          \
    libcap2-bin     \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Layer-cached dependency build: copy manifests first, dummy compile
WORKDIR /build
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \
    cargo build --release && \
    rm -rf src target/release/deps/rbid-* target/release/.fingerprint/rbid-*

# Real source build
COPY src/ src/
RUN cargo build --release

# Install binary to standard location with CAP_NET_RAW file capability.
# Container cap_add only populates the bounding set; non-root ifrit user
# needs file capabilities to get NET_RAW in effective set for raw sockets.
RUN cp target/release/rbid /usr/local/bin/rbid && \
    setcap cap_net_raw+ep /usr/local/bin/rbid

# Create unprivileged ifrit user
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash ifrit

# Keep the container alive with echo service on port 8888
CMD ["socat", "TCP-LISTEN:8888,fork,reuseaddr", "EXEC:cat"]

    netcat-openbsd  \
    traceroute      \
    strace          \
    libcap2-bin     \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Layer-cached dependency build: copy manifests first, dummy compile
WORKDIR /build
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \
    cargo build --release && \
    rm -rf src target/release/deps/rbid-* target/release/.fingerprint/rbid-*

# Real source build
COPY src/ src/
RUN cargo build --release

# Install binary to standard location with CAP_NET_RAW file capability.
# Container cap_add only populates the bounding set; non-root ifrit user
# needs file capabilities to get NET_RAW in effective set for raw sockets.
RUN cp target/release/rbid /usr/local/bin/rbid && \
    setcap cap_net_raw+ep /usr/local/bin/rbid

# Create unprivileged ifrit user
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash ifrit

# Keep the container alive with echo service on port 8888
CMD ["socat", "TCP-LISTEN:8888,fork,reuseaddr", "EXEC:cat"]

## Theurge & Ifrit: Crucible Security Testing

Read this file when working on theurge (test orchestrator), ifrit (attack binary), or crucible test cases. This is Recipe Bottle's internal test infrastructure only — Recipe Bottle does not require users to have Rust compilation tools.

### Architecture

Two Rust binaries with completely different roles and build targets:

- **Theurge** (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/`) — test orchestrator, runs on the **host** (macOS/Linux). Charges a crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle containers), invokes attacks, observes results, produces verdicts. Built via `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh`.
- **Ifrit** (`rbev-vessels/rbev-bottle-ifrit/`) — attack binary, runs **inside the bottle container**. Probes network security boundaries from the attacker's perspective. Built inside the Docker image during `docker build` — there is no host-side compilation, no cross-compile, no `cargo check` on macOS. The Dockerfile IS the build system.

**Coordinated tests** are the distinctive capability: theurge simultaneously observes from outside (via sentry writ/fiat commands) while ifrit attacks from inside. Neither binary alone can do this.

### Crucible Iteration Loop

The typical development cycle when changing ifrit or theurge code:

#### Iteration strategy: single cases first, full suite last

The full tadmor fixture takes ~10 minutes (charge + 50+ cases + quench). **Do not run it as the first verification step** — whether debugging failures or verifying new code. Instead:

1. Charge the crucible once
2. Run single-case against each new or changed test to verify it passes
3. Run the full fixture only after all targeted cases pass — as a final regression check
4. Quench

This applies to **all** crucible verification: new tests, bug fixes, refactors. The full suite is always the last step, never the first.

#### Full run (charge + all cases + quench in one command)

1. Edit ifrit source (`rbev-vessels/rbev-bottle-ifrit/src/`) or theurge source (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`)
2. If ifrit changed: kludge-rebuild the bottle image
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor
   ```
   This builds a new container image and drives the kludge hallmark into the nameplate's `rbrn.env`.
3. Git commit the hallmark change (kludge dirties `rbrn.env` — a clean working tree is required for charge, and the commit trail maintains audit integrity)
4. Run the full tadmor fixture:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-r.Run.tadmor.sh
   ```
   This charges the crucible, runs all cases, and quenches — one command.

#### Single-case debugging (manual charge/quench lifecycle)

When iterating on a specific failing test case:

1. Charge the crucible (starts containers, leaves them running):
   ```
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
2. Run individual cases against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.tadmor.sh case-name
   ```
   Omit the case name to list all available cases.
3. Edit code, rebuild as needed (kludge for ifrit, `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` for theurge), re-run the single case. Repeat.
4. Quench when done:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh
   ```

#### Ordaining after green

Kludge builds are for rapid local iteration. Once all tests pass with the kludge hallmark, ordain for a production-grade Cloud Build image:

1. Ordain the vessel:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit
   ```
2. Summon the ordained hallmark locally:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit <hallmark>
   ```
3. Drive the ordained hallmark into the nameplate (edit `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK` in `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`), commit, and re-run the full fixture to verify.

**Hallmark prefixes** tell you what you have: `k` = kludge (local build), `c` = conjured (Cloud Build ordained).

### Test Sections

Tadmor crucible cases are organized by test pattern:

| Section | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `tadmor-basic-infra` | Smoke tests — containers up, DNS responding | pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds |
| `tadmor-ifrit-attacks` | Single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only | dns-allowed, dns-blocked, apt blocked |
| `tadmor-observation` | Sentry-side observation of bottle behavior | iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation |
| `tadmor-correlated` | Theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result | tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests |
| `tadmor-sortie-attacks` | Multi-step ifrit sorties (complex attack sequences) | DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle |
| `tadmor-unilateral-novel` | Ifrit sorties testing novel attack vectors | route manipulation, subnet escape, DNAT reflection |
| `tadmor-coordinated-attacks` | Simultaneous attack + observation | ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability |
| `tadmor-coordinated-integrity` | Attack then verify sentry state unchanged | sentry integrity, DNS cache integrity, MAC flood |

### Adding a New Test

**New ifrit attack** (simple probe, single command):
1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
4. Register in appropriate section

**New ifrit sortie** (complex multi-step attack):
1. Add `pub fn sortie_name()` in `rbida_sorties.rs`
2. Add constant, enum variant, and dispatch in `rbida_attacks.rs` (same as above)
3. Add theurge case — sorties may need coordinated observation (writ/fiat before/after)
4. Register in appropriate section

**Crucible verification workflow** (applies to all new tests and changes):

1. Build theurge: `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` and run unit tests: `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh`
2. If ifrit source changed: kludge-rebuild, commit hallmark, then charge:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor    # builds image, drives hallmark into rbrn.env
   # commit the rbrn.env change (clean tree required for charge)
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
3. Verify each new/changed case individually against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.tadmor.sh rbtdrc_sortie_new_case_name
   ```
4. Only after all targeted cases pass, run the full fixture for regression:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-r.Run.tadmor.sh
   ```
   Note: this charges and quenches internally — quench the manual crucible first if one is active (`tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh`).

+    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
+
+# Grant CAP_NET_RAW to python3 so the unprivileged ifrit user can open
+# raw sockets (required for scapy).  cap_add in compose only places the
+# capability in the bounding set; file capabilities promote it into the
+# effective/permitted sets for non-root execution.
+RUN setcap cap_net_raw+eip /usr/bin/python3

 # Install Claude Code globally (npm, then clean cache)
 RUN npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code --omit=dev \

Fast suite: **15/15 passing** (handbook-render) plus earlier fixtures, all green.

Status before running kludge+charge+tadmor suite — I should pause and check on notch strategy:

**Current state**: All rename changes are staged/unstaged in working tree (clean rename sweep). Kludge dirties `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env` with the hallmark fingerprint, and the theurge-ifrit context doc notes that **charge requires a clean working tree**.

**Options**:

1. **Notch the rename sweep now** (₢A6AAv-affiliated), then kludge → hallmark lands on clean tree → notch hallmark separately → charge → tadmor suite. Clean two-commit history: rename, hallmark.
2. **Run kludge now on dirty tree** — kludge will succeed, but the hallmark update and the rename sweep tangle in one commit. Charge refuses dirty tree.

I recommend **option 1**. Notch now with coronet `A6AAv`, summary around "ifrit-symmetric-rename mechanical sweep: shared common-ifrit-context + rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether". Files:

- `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`

Proceed with notch, or prefer option 2?

attachment.snippet:
70	The iptables backend is pinned to legacy via `update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy` in the Dockerfile.
71	This selects the most documented, most predictable, and most battle-tested iptables codepath — the one that security professionals have audited for two decades.
72	
73	===== Slim Bottle Vessel
74	
75	The tadmor bottle's runtime function is a socat echo service on port 8888.
76	The minimal bottle vessel requires only `socat`.
77	
78	===== Ifrit Bottle Vessel
79	
80	The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under `rbev-vessels/`:
81	
82	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
83	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
84	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time forge fixture that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.
85	
86	The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/` (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses the forge as `FROM` base). The forge itself lives in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/`.
87	
88	The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing:
89	
90	[cols="1,3"]
91	|===
92	| Package | Function
93	
94	| `nodejs` + `npm`
95	| Claude Code runtime (Claude Code is a Node.js application)
...
93	| `strace`
94	| Syscall boundary mapping — lets the agent observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
95	Hard to replicate in pure Python.
96	
97	| `bash`, `curl`, `git`
98	| Claude Code operational dependencies
99	|===
100	
101	The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
102	The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
103	Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: connection to Anthropic API via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist, with no general egress.
104	First release is Anthropic-only; future releases may open access to additional LLM providers.
105	
106	==== Volume Mount Design
107	
108	The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:
109	
110	[cols="1,1,3"]
111	|===
...
117	This includes `.rbk/` regime files (infrastructure shape, not credentials — actual RBRA credential files reside outside the repository in the secrets directory).
118	
119	| Escape test directory (`rbtid/`)
120	| Read-write
121	| Persistent storage for attack scripts, probe results, and curated escape test modules.
122	Contents are committed to version control and maintained as part of the project's test infrastructure.
123	|===
124	
125	Volume mount configuration lives in per-nameplate compose fragments (e.g., `tadmor.compose.yml`, `moriah.compose.yml`) as relative paths, portable across Windows/Linux/macOS.
126	
127	// ============================================================================
128	// BASE IMAGE TRADE STUDY
129	// ============================================================================
130	
131	==== Base Image Selection
132	
133	Choosing the container base image for the sentry is a security decision, not a packaging convenience.

f4091b62 jjb:1011-600a5ef8:₢A6AAs:W: Added bounded 5xx retry to JWT SA and Payor OAuth access probes via new zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry helper (factored out of both probe_once sites per BCG load-bearing-complexity). Retry policy: 3 attempts with 2s→4s exponential backoff on 500/502/503/504, buc_die on exhausted retries or curl-network failure. RBSAJ and RBSAO specs amended with a new branch in step 4.iii.d Evaluate Response (before Otherwise), mirrored between JWT and OAuth probes. Verification: access-probe fixture 4/4 green; full service suite 80/80 green end-to-end (6 fixtures including four-mode-integration, which had transiently failed on an earlier run). The AAm trigger — a 503 bailing the suite before four-mode could execute — is now resolved; the suite rides through transient 5xx without operator intervention.
600a5ef8 jjb:1011-249f4162:₢A6AAw:W: Documentation skeleton for moriah nameplate landed bundled into AAv's commit 04f21003 (see commit message for explicit attribution of 'accompanying edit' / 'companion edit'). Surfaces touched: README #moriah anchor + parallel definitional prose + tadmor refinement (consuming Kludged Hallmarks for daily iteration framing) + 4 new directory rows (moriah/, common-ifrit-forge-context, ifrit-airgap, ifrit-forge, plus Dockerfile.airgap line); RBSIP three-vessel family expansion + tether/airgap nameplate split (moriah introduced paralleling tadmor) + moriah.compose.yml in compose-fragment example. RBS0 rbsi_moriah quoin skipped per docket judgment (nameplate moniker, not spec concept). RBSHR line 144 moriah row skipped per docket judgment (tadmor-specific CIDR config, not general adversarial testing). README line 191 glossary single-example preserved (Reference Nameplates section now lists both prominently). Anchors now in place to gate AAV Phase 2 handbook content; handbook tabtargets can hyperlink to ${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL}#moriah via buh_tlt. Working tree clean at wrap; this is a pure state-marker commit.
249f4162 jjb:1011-77456b4e:₢A6AAs:n: Add bounded 5xx retry to access probes via new zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry helper (factored per BCG load-bearing-complexity — same curl invariants, same failure class, same recovery across the two probe_once sites). Retry policy as kindle constants in rbgv: 3 attempts, initial 2s backoff doubling (2s→4s, worst-case +6s added latency). Helper takes label/url/token/resp-file/code-file/stderr-file; preserves all forensic stderr capture; buc_die on curl-network failure or exhausted 5xx retries; returns to caller for normal case-switch on any non-5xx response. Caller blocks in zrbgv_jwt_ar_probe_once and zrbgv_payor_crm_probe_once reduced to a single helper call — 200/401|403/other case-switches at call sites unchanged. RBSAJ and RBSAO specs amended with a new branch in step 4.iii.d Evaluate Response (before Otherwise): transient 5xx triggers retry with exponential backoff, buc_die after 3 repeated 5xx; existing 200/401/403/404 branches untouched. Verification: tt/rbtd-r.Run.access-probe.sh green 4/4 (healthy path through the helper). Service suite ran access-probe 4/4 (the AAm blocker is resolved) and now proceeds to four-mode, which hit a separate unrelated failure (rbtdrc_four_mode_supply_chain) — orthogonal to this pace, out of scope.

e29568f4 jjb:1011-f4091b62:₢A6AAv:n: Drive kludge hallmark k260416183104-f4091b62 into tadmor rbrn.env after ifrit-symmetric-rename kludge of rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether. Local build of new vessel name succeeded (rust:slim-bookworm base, layer cache intact, 9-step build, image+vouch tagged in GAR namespace under new sigil). Clean tree required for upcoming charge — capturing hallmark drive as separate commit from the rename sweep for audit clarity.
f4091b62 jjb:1011-600a5ef8:₢A6AAs:W: Added bounded 5xx retry to JWT SA and Payor OAuth access probes via new zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry helper (factored out of both probe_once sites per BCG load-bearing-complexity). Retry policy: 3 attempts with 2s→4s exponential backoff on 500/502/503/504, buc_die on exhausted retries or curl-network failure. RBSAJ and RBSAO specs amended with a new branch in step 4.iii.d Evaluate Response (before Otherwise), mirrored between JWT and OAuth probes. Verification: access-probe fixture 4/4 green; full service suite 80/80 green end-to-end (6 fixtures including four-mode-integration, which had transiently failed on an earlier run). The AAm trigger — a 503 bailing the suite before four-mode could execute — is now resolved; the suite rides through transient 5xx without operator intervention.
600a5ef8 jjb:1011-249f4162:₢A6AAw:W: Documentation skeleton for moriah nameplate landed bundled into AAv's commit 04f21003 (see commit message for explicit attribution of 'accompanying edit' / 'companion edit'). Surfaces touched: README #moriah anchor + parallel definitional prose + tadmor refinement (consuming Kludged Hallmarks for daily iteration framing) + 4 new directory rows (moriah/, common-ifrit-forge-context, ifrit-airgap, ifrit-forge, plus Dockerfile.airgap line); RBSIP three-vessel family expansion + tether/airgap nameplate split (moriah introduced paralleling tadmor) + moriah.compose.yml in compose-fragment example. RBS0 rbsi_moriah quoin skipped per docket judgment (nameplate moniker, not spec concept). RBSHR line 144 moriah row skipped per docket judgment (tadmor-specific CIDR config, not general adversarial testing). README line 191 glossary single-example preserved (Reference Nameplates section now lists both prominently). Anchors now in place to gate AAV Phase 2 handbook content; handbook tabtargets can hyperlink to ${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL}#moriah via buh_tlt. Working tree clean at wrap; this is a pure state-marker commit.
249f4162 jjb:1011-77456b4e:₢A6AAs:n: Add bounded 5xx retry to access probes via new zrbgv_http_get_with_5xx_retry helper (factored per BCG load-bearing-complexity — same curl invariants, same failure class, same recovery across the two probe_once sites). Retry policy as kindle constants in rbgv: 3 attempts, initial 2s backoff doubling (2s→4s, worst-case +6s added latency). Helper takes label/url/token/resp-file/code-file/stderr-file; preserves all forensic stderr capture; buc_die on curl-network failure or exhausted 5xx retries; returns to caller for normal case-switch on any non-5xx response. RBSAJ and RBSAO specs amended with a new branch in step 4.iii.d Evaluate Response (before Otherwise): transient 5xx triggers retry with exponential backoff, buc_die after 3 repeated 5xx; existing 200/401/403/404 branches untouched. Verification: tt/rbtd-r.Run.access-probe.sh green 4/4 (healthy path through the helper). Service suite ran access-probe 4/4 (the AAm blocker is resolved) and now proceeds to four-mode, which hit a separate unrelated failure (rbtdrc_four_mode_supply_chain) — orthogonal to this pace, out of scope.

# Adversarial ifrit testing environment

# Core Service Definition
RBRN_MONIKER=tadmor
RBRN_DESCRIPTION="Adversarial ifrit testing environment for AI escape test authoring"
RBRN_RUNTIME=docker

# Vessel Source Configuration (GAR vessels)
RBRN_SENTRY_VESSEL=rbev-sentry-deb-tether
RBRN_BOTTLE_VESSEL=rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether
RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=c260403180049-r260404010221
RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=k260416183104-f4091b62

# Port Service Configuration
RBRN_ENTRY_MODE=enabled
RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION=8890
RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE=8888

# Network Uplink Configuration
RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN=10000
RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE=allowlist
RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE=allowlist
RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS="93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12"
RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS="example.com example.org"

# Network Configuration
RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP=10.242.0.0
RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK=24
RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP=10.242.0.2
RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP=10.242.0.3

# eof

input.command:
cat > .claude/jjm/officia/260416-1011/gazette_in.md << 'SPEC'
# jjezs_slate tadmor-tcp-timeout-debug

## Character

Diagnostic investigation — narrow, focused, may require interactive crucible probing. The 3 failing cases (`rbtdrc_tcp443_allow_example`, `rbtdrc_cidr_all_ports_allowed`, `rbtdrc_sortie_http_end_to_end`) all hit the same root cause: TCP connect to `172.66.147.243` (Cloudflare IP for example.com) times out from inside the bottle. DNS resolves fine (ifrit DNS tests pass), iptables allows the CIDR (`93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12`), but the resolved IP `172.66.147.243` falls in `172.66.0.0/16` which is NOT in the allowed CIDR list. This may be a Cloudflare CDN rotation that moved example.com out of the previously-allowed ranges — or a sentry NAT/masquerade issue.

## Docket

Debug the TCP timeout affecting 3 tadmor security suite cases.

### Observed

- All 3 cases connect to `172.66.147.243` on ports 80 or 443
- DNS resolution succeeds (dig works, ifrit DNS tests all pass)
- Error: `nc: connect to 172.66.147.243 port 443 (tcp) timed out`
- Ifrit verdict: `FAIL BREACH` — ifrit interprets timeout as blocked traffic that should be allowed

### Hypotheses (ordered by likelihood)

1. **CIDR mismatch**: example.com now resolves to `172.66.147.243` which is in `172.66.0.0/16`, outside the tadmor allowlist CIDRs `93.184.216.0/24` and `104.16.0.0/12`. The IP rotated via Cloudflare CDN. Fix: update `RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS` in tadmor rbrn.env.
2. **NAT masquerade path broken**: sentry masquerade rule not applying to this IP range. Investigate iptables nat table.
3. **Host-side network issue**: Docker Desktop routing problem for this specific IP range. Test from host to confirm reachability.

### Steps

1. From host: `curl -sI https://example.com` and `dig +short example.com` — confirm current IP and reachability
2. Charge tadmor, shell into sentry (`rbw-ch`), inspect iptables nat/filter rules for the resolved IP
3. Shell into bottle (`rbw-cr`), attempt `nc -zv <resolved-ip> 443` manually
4. If hypothesis 1 confirmed: update `RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS` to cover new range, re-test
5. If hypothesis 2: trace iptables counters for the specific connection
6. Run the 3 failing cases via SingleCase to confirm fix
SPEC

content.text: ₢A6AAy

Slated as **₢A6AAy** `tadmor-tcp-timeout-debug`.

Key observation already in the docket: the resolved IP `172.66.147.243` is in `172.66.0.0/16`, which falls **outside** tadmor's allowed CIDRs (`93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12`). Looks like Cloudflare rotated example.com to a different range. Hypothesis 1 (CIDR mismatch) is likely — the sentry is correctly blocking traffic to an IP that isn't in the allowlist, and ifrit is reporting that as a breach because the test assumes example.com resolves inside the allowed range.

content.text:
Found it. Commit `8b39ea53` (₢A6AAG) fixed exactly this problem — **but only for the ccyolo nameplate**:

```diff
- RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS="160.79.104.0/23 93.184.216.0/24"
+ RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS="160.79.104.0/23 104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13"
```

The comment explains: *"example.com is currently behind Cloudflare (104.16.0.0/12, 172.64.0.0/13)"*. `172.66.147.243` falls in `172.64.0.0/13`.

**Tadmor** never got the `172.64.0.0/13` CIDR. It still has:
```
RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS="93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12"
```

Missing `172.64.0.0/13`. That's the bug — the Cloudflare rotation fix was applied to ccyolo but not propagated to tadmor.

- 4. If hypothesis 1 confirmed: update `RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS` to cover new range, re-test
- 5. If hypothesis 2: trace iptables counters for the specific connection
- 6. Run the 3 failing cases via SingleCase to confirm fix
+ Fix propagated from ccyolo nameplate (8b39ea53, ₢A6AAG) where the same Cloudflare rotation was resolved by adding `172.64.0.0/13`. Tadmor was missed in that pass.

content.text: Done. ₢A6AAy reslated from diagnostic investigation to verify-only. Fix committed at `8420c17d`, verification deferred to when you reach that pace.

- Should be a benign test target (not a production service we'd be hammering)

4. Do IANA's example domains (example.com/org/net) have stable IPs when accessed directly (not via CDN)?

Identical IPs across all three major public DNS resolvers. Here are the findings:

---

**The problem**: example.com moved behind Cloudflare. The current tadmor allowlist (`93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13`) whitelists ~1.57M IPs. The old Edgecast IP 93.184.216.34 no longer responds at all.

**Top candidate: www.internic.net (ICANN-owned)**

- IP: `192.0.46.9` -- stable across Google/Cloudflare/Quad9 DNS, CNAME to `internicwww.vip.icann.org`
- CIDR: `192.0.32.0/20` (4,096 IPs, owned by ICANN directly)
- Responds HTTP 200 on both port 80 and 443
- Plain Apache, no CDN (no `cf-ray`, no `x-cache` headers)
- ICANN operates the internet's root infrastructure; this domain has existed since 1993
- **383x smaller allowlist** than current Cloudflare ranges

**Runner-up: www.iana.org (also ICANN-owned)**

- IP: `192.0.33.8` -- same `192.0.32.0/20` block, same stability characteristics
- Both ports, plain Apache, no CDN
- Same CIDR as internic.net, so using either (or both) requires only `192.0.32.0/20`

**Honorable mention: connectivity-check.ubuntu.com (Canonical)**

- IPs in two blocks: `91.189.88.0/21` (2,048 IPs) + `185.125.188.0/22` (1,024 IPs)
- Returns HTTP 204 (purpose-built connectivity check)
- Slightly more complex: two CIDR ranges needed, and Canonical could restructure
- Still far better than Cloudflare ranges

**Recommendation**: Replace example.com with `www.internic.net` and use `192.0.32.0/20` as the single allowed CIDR. ICANN literally runs the internet naming system -- these IPs are as stable as anything gets. The domain is an informational reference site (not a production service), appropriate for test traffic. The tests referencing `example.com` in DNS queries would need updating in `rbida_sorties.rs`, `rbida_attacks.rs`, `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`, and the allowed-domains in `rbrn.env`.

input.command:
cat > .claude/jjm/officia/260416-1011/gazette_in.md << 'SPEC'
# jjezs_slate replace-example-com-with-internic

## Character

Cross-cutting infrastructure change — touches ifrit attack source, theurge orchestrator, both nameplates (tadmor + ccyolo), and needs a spec documentation home in RBS0. Not mechanically hard but the blast radius spans Rust source, regime config, and documentation. Requires a crucible verification cycle for both nameplates.

## Docket

Replace `example.com` with `www.internic.net` as the test connectivity target across the entire security testing infrastructure. Replace the large Cloudflare CIDR allowlist entries with the much smaller ICANN block. Document the rationale in RBS0.

### Problem

example.com moved behind Cloudflare CDN, causing its resolved IPs to rotate across huge ranges. The current allowlist (`93.184.216.0/24 104.16.0.0/12 172.64.0.0/13`) whitelists ~1.57M IPs just to reach one test domain. For a security testing framework where containment is the core value proposition, this is an unacceptable hole. The old IANA-owned IP `93.184.216.34` no longer responds.

### Alternatives considered

| Domain | IP range | CIDR size | CDN | Notes |
|--------|----------|-----------|-----|-------|
| `example.com` (current) | 172.66.x.x, 104.16.x.x | /12 + /13 = ~1.57M IPs | Cloudflare | Rotates unpredictably, triggered this investigation |
| `www.internic.net` | 192.0.46.9 | 192.0.32.0/20 = 4,096 IPs | None (plain Apache) | ICANN-owned since 1993, root infrastructure |
| `www.iana.org` | 192.0.33.8 | Same 192.0.32.0/20 block | None | Also ICANN; same CIDR as internic |
| `connectivity-check.ubuntu.com` | 91.189.88.x + 185.125.188.x | /21 + /22 = ~3K IPs | None | Purpose-built connectivity check (HTTP 204), but two CIDRs needed, Canonical could restructure |

### Decision

**`www.internic.net`** with CIDR `192.0.32.0/20`. ICANN literally runs the internet naming system — these IPs are as stable as anything gets. The domain is an informational reference site (not a production service), appropriate for periodic test traffic. Single CIDR, 383x smaller than current Cloudflare ranges. Responds on both port 80 and 443.

### Touch sites

1. `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`: replace example.com CIDRs and domains with internic
2. `.rbk/ccyolo/rbrn.env`: same replacement
3. `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_attacks.rs`: hardcoded example.com in DNS/TCP/HTTP attack selectors
4. `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs`: example.com in sortie scripts
5. `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs`: theurge test case domain references
6. RBS0 or companion spec: document test target selection rationale and the CIDR discipline (exact placement TBD during pace)
7. Grep sweep for remaining `example.com` references in test infrastructure

### Verification

1. Kludge ifrit-tether (source changed), drive hallmark into tadmor
2. Charge + Run.tadmor — all 54 cases green (especially the 3 previously-failing TCP cases)
3. Charge + verify ccyolo connectivity (if ccyolo has example.com-dependent tests)
4. Fast suite green
5. Zero-hit grep for `example.com` in rbrn.env + Rust source (DNS test domains like `example.org` in blocked-DNS tests may intentionally remain — they don't need CIDR allowlisting since they're testing DNS resolution, not TCP connectivity)
SPEC
